The concept of 'degrowth' challenges the fundamental assumption that underpins modern economics: that perpetual economic growth is both possible and desirable. Proponents of degrowth argue that on a finite planet with finite resources, infinite growth is a mathematical impossibility. They point to the 'Jevons paradox' — the observation that technological improvements in resource efficiency often lead to increased, rather than decreased, total resource consumption, because efficiency gains reduce costs and stimulate demand. For example, more fuel-efficient cars make driving cheaper per kilometer, leading people to drive more, often offsetting the efficiency gains entirely. Degrowth advocates propose an alternative economic model focused on reducing consumption, shortening work hours, prioritizing well-being over GDP, and redistributing wealth more equitably. Critics dismiss degrowth as politically unrealistic and economically dangerous, arguing that economic contraction would cause widespread unemployment and hardship, disproportionately affecting the poorest members of society.
exercises.question: What is the Jevons paradox?
options: A) Technology always reduces consumption
B) Efficiency improvements often increase total consumption because lower costs stimulate more demand
C) Technology makes resources infinite
D) Economic growth is always sustainable
exercises.explanation_pt: O paradoxo de Jevons é que 'technological improvements in efficiency often lead to increased total consumption, because efficiency gains reduce costs and stimulate demand'.

exercises.passage: [Mesmo texto]
exercises.question: What do critics say about degrowth?
options: A) It's politically unrealistic and could cause unemployment and hardship
B) It would only affect rich countries
C) It's the best economic model
D) It has been successfully implemented
exercises.explanation_pt: Críticos 'dismiss degrowth as politically unrealistic and economically dangerous, arguing that contraction would cause widespread unemployment and hardship'.

exercises.passage: Amartya Sen's 'capability approach' to development, which earned him the Nobel Prize in Economics in 1998, redefines what it means for a society to develop. Traditional measures of development — GDP per capita, industrialization rates, technological advancement — focus on economic output. Sen argues these measures are inadequate because they tell us nothing about whether people can actually live lives they value. A country with high GDP might still have widespread illiteracy, gender inequality, and political repression. The capability approach defines development as the expansion of people's substantive freedoms — their ability to be healthy, educated, politically active, and socially connected. This framework has profoundly influenced international development policy, leading to the creation of the United Nations Human Development Index (HDI), which measures development through life expectancy, education, and income rather than GDP alone.
exercises.question: Why does Sen consider GDP an inadequate measure of development?
options: A) GDP measures economic output but says nothing about whether people can live lives they value
B) GDP is too difficult to calculate
C) GDP only measures rich countries
D) GDP is always inaccurate
exercises.explanation_pt: Sen argumenta que GDP é inadequado porque 'tells us nothing about whether people can actually live lives they value' — um país com alto GDP pode ter analfabetismo, desigualdade e repressão.

exercises.passage: [Mesmo texto]
exercises.question: What did the capability approach lead to?
options: A) The creation of the UN Human Development Index (HDI)
B) Higher GDP targets
C) The abolition of GDP
D) Less foreign aid
exercises.explanation_pt: A abordagem de Sen 'led to the creation of the United Nations Human Development Index (HDI), which measures development through life expectancy, education, and income rather than GDP alone'.

exercises.passage: The 'resource curse' — also known as the 'paradox of plenty' — describes the puzzling observation that countries rich in natural resources, particularly oil, minerals, and gas, often experience slower economic growth, higher corruption, more authoritarian governance, and greater inequality than resource-poor countries. Nigeria, Venezuela, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo are frequently cited examples: despite enormous mineral wealth, their populations remain among the poorest in the world. Several mechanisms explain this paradox. Resource revenues flow directly to governments, reducing their dependence on taxation — and therefore their accountability to citizens. When governments don't need tax revenue from citizens, they have less incentive to invest in education, healthcare, and infrastructure. Additionally, resource wealth attracts rent-seeking behavior: powerful individuals and groups compete to control resource revenues rather than engaging in productive economic activity. The 'Dutch disease' compounds the problem: resource exports strengthen the currency, making other exports uncompetitive and hollowing out the manufacturing and agricultural sectors.
exercises.question: Why does the resource curse reduce government accountability?
options: A) Resources cause corruption automatically
B) Resources make governments too busy
C) Citizens don't want accountability
D) When governments don't depend on citizen taxes, they have less incentive to serve citizens
exercises.explanation_pt: 'When governments don't need tax revenue from citizens, they have less incentive to invest in education, healthcare, and infrastructure' — sem dependência de impostos, sem accountability.

exercises.instruction_pt: Leia o texto acadêmico e responda:
exercises.question: According to the passage, what is the 'overview effect'?
options: A) A cognitive bias that causes astronauts to forget their training in space
B) A profound cognitive shift in awareness reported by astronauts viewing Earth from space
C) A medical condition caused by prolonged exposure to zero gravity environments
D) A psychological theory that has been disproven by recent space research
exercises.passage: The 'overview effect' is a cognitive shift in awareness reported by astronauts during spaceflight. First described by author Frank White in 1987, the phenomenon occurs when individuals view Earth from orbit or from the lunar surface. Astronauts consistently describe a profound sense of the planet's fragility, the artificiality of political boundaries, and an overwhelming connection to all life on Earth. Astronaut Edgar Mitchell described his experience as 'an explosion of awareness' and 'an overwhelming sense of oneness and connectedness.' Research in the field of neuroaesthetics has begun to investigate the neurological basis of this phenomenon. Studies using virtual reality simulations of spaceflight have shown measurable changes in participants' brain activity, particularly in regions associated with awe, self-transcendence, and environmental concern. Participants who experienced simulated overview effects reported significantly increased pro-environmental attitudes and behaviors that persisted for weeks after the experience. These findings have practical implications beyond space exploration. Environmental psychologists are exploring whether virtual overview experiences could be used as a tool for fostering environmental awareness and sustainable behavior in the general population. Preliminary studies suggest that even brief VR exposures to Earth-from-space perspectives can reduce psychological distance from environmental issues and increase willingness to engage in pro-environmental actions. However, critics caution that the overview effect experienced by actual astronauts involves genuine existential risk, physical displacement, and extended duration — factors that cannot be replicated through simulation.

exercises.question: What practical application of the overview effect are researchers exploring?
options: A) Training astronauts to resist the disorienting effects of space travel
B) Using VR simulations to foster environmental awareness in the general population
C) Developing new spacecraft that prevent the overview effect from occurring
D) Creating medications that replicate the neurological changes seen in astronauts

theme: TOEFL Reading — Filosofia da Ciência avançada

exercises.passage: The demarcation problem — distinguishing science from non-science — has occupied philosophers since the ancient Greeks. Karl Popper proposed falsifiability as the criterion: a theory is scientific if it makes predictions that could, in principle, be proven wrong. Evolutionary theory is scientific because it predicts specific fossil patterns that could potentially falsify it. Astrology, by contrast, is unfalsifiable because its predictions are so vague that any outcome can be interpreted as confirmation. Thomas Kuhn challenged Popper by arguing that scientists rarely abandon theories when faced with contradictory evidence — instead, they treat anomalies as puzzles to be solved within the existing paradigm. Theories are abandoned only during revolutionary 'paradigm shifts,' which are driven as much by social factors as by evidence. More recently, philosopher Larry Laudan has argued that the demarcation problem is insoluble — that there is no single criterion that reliably separates science from pseudoscience. Instead, he suggests evaluating claims on a case-by-case basis using multiple criteria: empirical testability, track record of solving problems, internal consistency, and compatibility with well-established knowledge.
exercises.question: What is the demarcation problem?
options: A) Distinguishing science from non-science
B) Separating physics from biology
C) Defining what counts as a fact
D) Drawing borders between countries
exercises.explanation_pt: O problema da demarcação é 'distinguishing science from non-science' = distinguir ciência de não-ciência.

exercises.passage: [Mesmo texto]
exercises.question: How did Kuhn challenge Popper?
options: A) He agreed completely with Popper
B) He argued scientists rarely abandon theories due to contradictory evidence — paradigm shifts are driven by social factors too
C) He rejected all of science
D) He said falsifiability is perfect
exercises.explanation_pt: Kuhn desafiou Popper argumentando que 'scientists rarely abandon theories when faced with contradictory evidence' — mudanças são 'driven as much by social factors as by evidence'.

exercises.passage: The precautionary principle — the idea that when an activity raises threats of harm to human health or the environment, precautionary measures should be taken even if some cause-and-effect relationships are not fully established scientifically — has become a cornerstone of environmental policy in Europe. The principle effectively shifts the burden of proof: instead of requiring evidence of harm before regulating a substance or activity, it requires evidence of safety before allowing it. Proponents argue this approach is essential for protecting public health from risks that are difficult to study — the long-term effects of microplastics, for instance, may take decades to fully understand, by which time irreversible damage may have occurred. Critics, particularly in the United States, argue that the precautionary principle stifles innovation by requiring proof of safety that may be impossible to provide — no product can ever be proven 100% safe. They prefer a risk-benefit approach: allowing activities unless evidence demonstrates that the risks outweigh the benefits.
exercises.question: How does the precautionary principle shift the burden of proof?
options: A) From industry to government
B) From requiring evidence of harm TO requiring evidence of safety before allowing an activity
C) It doesn't shift anything
D) From scientists to politicians
exercises.explanation_pt: O princípio 'shifts the burden of proof: instead of requiring evidence of harm before regulating, it requires evidence of safety before allowing' = em vez de provar dano, deve-se provar segurança.

exercises.passage: [Mesmo texto]
exercises.question: What do critics in the US argue against the precautionary principle?
options: A) It's too weak
B) It stifles innovation because proving 100% safety is impossible
C) It's only for Europe
D) It doesn't protect health
exercises.explanation_pt: Críticos americanos argumentam que 'the precautionary principle stifles innovation by requiring proof of safety that may be impossible to provide — no product can ever be proven 100% safe'.

exercises.passage: The replication crisis has exposed fundamental problems in how scientific research is conducted, published, and incentivized. A landmark 2015 study attempted to replicate 100 psychology experiments published in top journals; only 36% produced the same results. Similar problems have been found in medicine, economics, and other fields. Several systemic factors contribute. Publication bias means journals strongly prefer positive results — studies finding 'no effect' are rarely published, creating a distorted literature. P-hacking — the practice of analyzing data in multiple ways until a statistically significant result is found — inflates false positive rates. Small sample sizes produce unstable results that fail to replicate. And the 'publish or perish' culture incentivizes quantity over quality, pushing researchers to pursue novel, publishable findings rather than careful replication of existing work. Solutions being implemented include pre-registration of studies, registered reports, open data sharing, and changes to hiring criteria that value replication alongside original research.
exercises.question: What is the main cause of the replication crisis according to the passage?
options: A) Scientists are dishonest
B) Multiple systemic factors: publication bias, p-hacking, small samples, and publish-or-perish culture
C) Research methods are too advanced
D) Psychology is not a real science
exercises.explanation_pt: A crise é causada por múltiplos fatores sistêmicos: 'publication bias, p-hacking, small sample sizes, and publish or perish culture' — não desonestidade individual.

exercises.passage: The discovery of 'mirror neurons' — brain cells that fire both when performing an action and when observing someone else perform the same action — has been called one of the most important findings in neuroscience. Discovered by Giacomo Rizzolatti in macaque monkeys in the 1990s, mirror neurons appear to create an internal simulation of observed actions, potentially enabling us to understand others' intentions by mentally 'replaying' their movements in our own neural circuitry. Some researchers believe mirror neurons are the biological basis of empathy — our ability to feel what others feel. Vilayanur Ramachandran famously predicted that 'mirror neurons will do for psychology what DNA did for biology.' However, the field has become increasingly controversial. Critics argue that the mirror neuron hypothesis has been dramatically overstated. Direct evidence for mirror neurons in humans remains limited — most studies use brain imaging, which measures regional activity rather than individual neurons. Furthermore, the leap from 'neurons that simulate observed actions' to 'the basis of empathy, language, and civilization' involves speculative reasoning that goes far beyond the available evidence.
exercises.question: Why are mirror neurons controversial?
options: A) Direct evidence in humans is limited, and claims about their role in empathy may be overstated
B) They only work in monkeys
C) They don't exist
D) They cause disease
exercises.explanation_pt: A controvérsia é que 'direct evidence for mirror neurons in humans remains limited' e 'the leap from neurons that simulate actions to the basis of empathy involves speculative reasoning beyond the evidence'.

exercises.passage: [Mesmo texto]
exercises.question: What did Ramachandran predict about mirror neurons?
options: A) They would be disproven
B) They would do for psychology what DNA did for biology
C) They would cure diseases
D) They would replace brain imaging
exercises.explanation_pt: Ramachandran previu que 'mirror neurons will do for psychology what DNA did for biology' = teriam impacto revolucionário na psicologia.

exercises.passage: The gut-brain axis represents one of the most exciting frontiers in neuroscience. The human gut contains approximately 500 million neurons — more than the spinal cord — and produces over 90% of the body's serotonin, a neurotransmitter crucial for mood regulation. The gut microbiome — the trillions of bacteria living in our intestines — communicates with the brain through the vagus nerve, immune system signaling, and metabolic products. Groundbreaking studies have shown that germ-free mice (raised without any gut bacteria) display abnormal anxiety and social behavior, which can be reversed by introducing specific bacterial strains. In humans, preliminary research has linked gut microbiome composition to conditions including depression, anxiety, autism, and Parkinson's disease. Some researchers are exploring 'psychobiotics' — specific probiotic strains that may improve mental health. While the field is still in its infancy, the gut-brain axis may fundamentally change how we understand and treat mental illness, shifting from a brain-only model to one that considers the entire body as an interconnected system.
exercises.question: What percentage of the body's serotonin is produced in the gut?
options: D) Over 90%
exercises.explanation_pt: O intestino 'produces over 90% of the body's serotonin' = produz mais de 90% da serotonina do corpo.

options: A) Psychological tests
B) Anti-psychotic drugs
C) Brain surgeries
D) Specific probiotic strains that may improve mental health
exercises.explanation_pt: Psicobióticos são 'specific probiotic strains that may improve mental health' = cepas probióticas específicas que podem melhorar saúde mental.

options: A) By shifting from a brain-only model to one that considers the entire body as interconnected
B) It won't change anything
C) By eliminating the need for therapy
D) By replacing all medication
exercises.explanation_pt: O eixo intestino-cérebro pode 'fundamentally change how we understand and treat mental illness, shifting from a brain-only model to one that considers the entire body as an interconnected system'.

exercises.question: What is CRISPR-Cas9 as described in the passage?
options: A) A type of virus that causes genetic diseases in human populations
B) A gene-editing technology that allows precise modification of DNA sequences
C) A pharmaceutical drug used to treat hereditary conditions in patients
D) A computer algorithm designed to analyze genetic data from blood samples
exercises.passage: CRISPR-Cas9, a revolutionary gene-editing technology, has transformed the landscape of molecular biology since its development as a genome engineering tool in 2012. The system, adapted from a natural defense mechanism used by bacteria against viral infections, allows scientists to make precise modifications to DNA sequences in living organisms. The technology works by using a guide RNA molecule to direct the Cas9 enzyme to a specific location in the genome, where it creates a targeted double-strand break. The cell's natural repair mechanisms then either disrupt the targeted gene or incorporate a new DNA sequence. The potential medical applications are vast. Researchers have used CRISPR to correct genetic mutations responsible for sickle cell disease, muscular dystrophy, and certain forms of blindness in laboratory settings. In 2023, the first CRISPR-based therapy — Casgevy, for sickle cell disease — received regulatory approval in the United Kingdom and United States. However, the technology raises profound ethical questions. The possibility of editing human germline cells — changes that would be inherited by future generations — has sparked intense debate. In 2018, Chinese scientist He Jiankui announced the birth of twin girls whose genomes he had edited using CRISPR, provoking international condemnation. The scientific community has broadly agreed that germline editing for reproductive purposes is premature given current understanding of off-target effects — unintended modifications at other genomic locations. Governance frameworks for CRISPR technology remain fragmented, with different countries adopting varying regulatory approaches.

exercises.question: What ethical concern does the passage highlight about CRISPR technology?
options: A) That CRISPR is too expensive for most research laboratories to use effectively
B) That editing germline cells creates inheritable changes with unknown consequences
C) That CRISPR can only be used on bacterial organisms and not on human cells
D) That the technology was stolen from Chinese researchers without proper attribution

exercises.passage:
The concept of 'epistemic humility' — the recognition that our knowledge is always incomplete and our beliefs may be wrong — has gained renewed attention in an era of information overload and polarized public discourse. Unlike intellectual humility, which concerns an individual's relationship to their own ideas, epistemic humility operates at a broader level, acknowledging the fundamental limitations of human understanding itself.

Philosophically, epistemic humility draws on the Socratic tradition — the idea that wisdom begins with recognizing one's own ignorance. In science, it manifests as the understanding that all knowledge is provisional and subject to revision. The history of science is littered with theories that were once considered settled — the geocentric model, phlogiston theory, the static universe — all of which were eventually overturned by new evidence. This history should instill humility in anyone who claims to have definitive knowledge about complex phenomena.

Practically, epistemic humility has important implications for public policy and democratic discourse. Policymakers who recognize the limits of their knowledge are more likely to implement policies incrementally, gather evidence, and adjust course when results don't match expectations — an approach sometimes called 'evidence-based policy.' In contrast, leaders who are certain they are right are more likely to implement sweeping changes without adequate testing, ignore contradictory evidence, and double down on failed approaches.

Critics argue that excessive epistemic humility can lead to paralysis — if we acknowledge that we might be wrong about everything, how can we act decisively? This is a legitimate concern. Epistemic humility does not mean treating all claims as equally valid or refusing to act until certainty is achieved. Rather, it means holding our beliefs with appropriate confidence — strong enough to act on, but flexible enough to revise when evidence demands it. As the philosopher Bertrand Russell put it: 'The fundamental cause of the trouble is that in the modern world the stupid are cocksure while the intelligent are full of doubt.'

exercises.explanation_pt: O argumento principal é que humildade epistêmica — reconhecer que nosso conhecimento é incompleto — é essencial para boa ciência e governança.

exercises.passage:
The question of whether consciousness can be explained by physical processes alone — or whether it requires something beyond physics — remains the deepest unsolved problem in philosophy and neuroscience. Materialists argue that consciousness is an emergent property of complex neural computation: just as wetness emerges from the interaction of water molecules without any single molecule being 'wet,' consciousness emerges from the interaction of billions of neurons without any single neuron being 'conscious.' This view is supported by the observation that specific changes to the brain — through injury, drugs, or stimulation — produce predictable changes in conscious experience.

Dualists counter that no amount of physical description can capture the subjective quality of experience — what philosopher Thomas Nagel called 'what it is like' to have an experience. You can describe every physical process involved in seeing the color red — the wavelength of light, the activation of retinal cells, the neural pathways to the visual cortex — without ever explaining why this particular physical process feels like anything at all. This is David Chalmers' 'hard problem of consciousness': why is there subjective experience associated with physical processes?

A third position, panpsychism, has gained surprising traction among philosophers in recent years. Panpsychism proposes that consciousness is not something that emerges from complex systems but is a fundamental feature of reality itself — present, in some rudimentary form, in all matter. Just as mass and charge are intrinsic properties of particles, consciousness may be an intrinsic property that becomes more complex as physical systems become more organized. While this idea sounds radical, proponents argue it avoids the hard problem entirely: if consciousness is fundamental rather than emergent, there is no mysterious 'gap' between physical processes and subjective experience.

Each position faces serious challenges. Materialism must explain why physical processes produce subjective experience at all. Dualism must explain how non-physical consciousness interacts with the physical brain. And panpsychism must explain the 'combination problem': how do the micro-consciousnesses of individual particles combine to form the unified conscious experience of a human mind?
exercises.question: (Q1 — Main idea) What is the passage primarily about?
options: A) How the brain works

exercises.explanation_pt: O texto apresenta três posições filosóficas sobre consciência (materialismo, dualismo, pampsiquismo) e os desafios que cada uma enfrenta.

B) Building conscious AI

exercises.explanation_pt: O hard problem de Chalmers: 'why is there subjective experience associated with physical processes?' = por que processos físicos produzem experiência subjetiva?
